subject: Physics
solution:
Magnitude of magnetic field due to semi-infinite portion of wire AB at point C is

B1=μ0i4πr

Magnitude of magnetic field due to semicircular arc BD at point C is

B2=μ0i4r

Magnitude of magnetic field due to semi-infinite portion DE at point C is 0.

Using right-hand rule, it can be seen that directions of B1→ and B2→ are both outwards from the plane.

Therefore, the magnitude of net magnetic field at point C is

B=B1+B2

   =μ0i2r12+12π

subject: Physics
solution:
The applied magnetic field provides the required centripetal force to the charge particle, so it can move in circular path of radius $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}$
$$
\therefore \quad B q v=\frac{m^2}{d / 2}
$$
or, $\quad B=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qd}}$
Time interval for which a uniform magnetic field is applied
$$
\Delta \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}}{\mathrm{v}}
$$
(particle reverses its direction after time $\Delta \mathrm{t}$ by covering semi circle).
$$
\Delta \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}}{2 \mathrm{v}}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
From the figure, it is evident that the magnetic field at O due to the straight portion of the loop is zero, only the contributions from the circular parts survive.

Also, the direction of the magnetic field due to the circular loops can be determined by the right-hand corkscrew rule.

Hence, the net magnetic field at O can be calculated as follows:

B=μ0i2R2π2π⊗+μ0i2R1π2π⊗=μ0i4R2+μ0i4R1⊗=4π×10-7×44×4π+4π×10-7×44×2π⊗ T=3×10-7⊗ T

Hence, α=3.

solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic force on the conductor is given by

FB= ILB   ...1

The formula to calculate the gravitational force on the conductor is given by

FG= mg   ...2

Equate both the equations and simplify to obtain the required current through the conductor.

ILB=mg⇒I= mgLB   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required current through the conductor.

I=401000 kg×10 m s-20.5 m×0.40 T=2 A

solution:
Non-polar materials do not have any permanent dipole moment. This statement is true.

When a non-polar material is placed in an electric field, centre of positive charge is pushed in the direction of the electric field and centre of the negative charge is pushed in the direction opposite to the electric field. Hence, they don't coincide. Therefore, reason is false.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the number of turns per unit radial width be $n = \dfrac{N}{r_2 - r_1}$.

Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$. The number of turns in this element is $dN = n \, dr$.

The magnetic moment of this elemental ring is $dM = I \, dN \, A = I \left( \dfrac{N}{r_2 - r_1} \, dr \right) (\pi r^2)$.

Integrating from $r_1$ to $r_2$ to find the total magnetic moment $M$:

$M = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{I \pi N}{r_2 - r_1} r^2 \, dr = \dfrac{I \pi N}{3(r_2 - r_1)} (r_2^3 - r_1^3)$

Substituting the given values: $I = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $N = 200$, $r_1 = 0.03$ m, and $r_2 = 0.06$ m:

$M = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times \pi \times 200}{3(0.06 - 0.03)} \left( (0.06)^3 - (0.03)^3 \right)$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi}{0.09} (216 \times 10^{-6} - 27 \times 10^{-6})$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi}{0.09} \times 189 \times 10^{-6} = 4\pi \times 2100 \times 10^{-6}$

$M = 84\pi \times 10^{-4} \approx 84 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} = 263.76 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.64 \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$, we get $\alpha = 2.64$.

Answer: $2.64$

subject: Physics
solution:
For the first figure, the magnetic field at the centre P is the vector sum of the magnetic fields due to two semi-infinite straight wires and one semicircular arc.

The magnetic field due to the top semi-infinite wire at P is $B_{1a} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The magnetic field due to the bottom semi-infinite wire at P is $B_{1b} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The magnetic field due to the semicircular arc at P is $B_{1c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4r} = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The total magnetic field at P is $B_1 = B_{1a} + B_{1b} + B_{1c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + 1 + \pi) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (2 + \pi)$.

For the second figure, the magnetic field at the centre Q is the vector sum of the magnetic fields due to one horizontal semi-infinite wire, one semicircular arc, and one vertical semi-infinite wire.

The magnetic field due to the horizontal semi-infinite wire at Q is $B_{2a} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The magnetic field due to the semicircular arc at Q is $B_{2c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4r} = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

Since the arc is semicircular, it ends at a point directly below Q. The vertical semi-infinite wire starts from this point and extends downwards, meaning its line of action passes through Q. Therefore, the magnetic field due to this vertical wire at Q is $B_{2b} = 0$.

The total magnetic field at Q is $B_2 = B_{2a} + B_{2b} + B_{2c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + 0 + \pi) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + \pi)$.

The ratio of the magnetic fields is $\dfrac{B_1}{B_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (2 + \pi)}{\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + \pi)} = \dfrac{2 + \pi}{1 + \pi}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{2 + \pi}{1 + \pi}$
